past_medical_history: Hypertension, diagnosed 8 years ago, well-controlled on medication
Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 5 years ago, managed with metformin, HbA1c 6.8%
Migraine with visual aura in her 30s-40s, resolved in menopause
Bilateral cataract surgery 3 years ago with intraocular lens implantation
family_history: Mother alive at 89, has mild memory difficulties attributed to aging
Father died at 74 from complications of a stroke
Older brother, age 70, has type 2 diabetes and hypertension
No known family history of early-onset dementia

social_history.occupation: Retired civil engineer, worked for 35 years at a municipal planning office, retired 2 years ago
social_history.living_situation: Lives with husband in a two-story condominium
social_history.alcohol: Social drinker, 1-2 glasses of wine per week
social_history.exercise: Previously enjoyed hiking and swimming; has become less active due to concerns about spatial orientation on trails

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, date, and situation. MMSE 27/30, losing points on intersecting pentagons (unable to accurately copy the figure), serial sevens (1 error), and one orientation sub-item (day of week). Clock drawing is notable for poor spatial planning — numbers are crowded into the right half of the clock face, though the correct time is set. Delayed recall is 2/3 at 5 minutes, improving to 3/3 with categorical cueing. Naming intact. Repetition intact. Comprehension intact for multi-step commands. Reading aloud is intact but she reports difficulty following lines of text on a page. Calculations are performed slowly but accurately.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, reactive to light. Pseudophakic bilaterally. Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. Visual fields full to confrontation by finger counting, but simultaneous bilateral visual stimulation reveals left-sided visual extinction. Face symmetric. Hearing grossly intact. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius 5/5 bilaterally. Tongue midline.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk and tone normal. Strength 5/5 in all extremities. No pronator drift. No tremor, rigidity, or bradykinesia.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch, pinprick, temperature, vibration, and proprioception intact in all extremities. Cortical sensory testing reveals difficulty with graphesthesia in the left hand (identifies 2 of 5 numbers correctly, right hand 5/5). Stereognosis impaired in the left hand (unable to identify a key by touch, correctly identifies a coin; right hand intact).
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric throughout. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing intact bilaterally. Heel-to-shin intact. Rapid alternating movements normal. However, patient exhibits optic ataxia — she has difficulty reaching accurately for objects placed in her left peripheral visual field, overshooting on several attempts.
neurological_exam.gait: Steady gait with normal base and stride. Tandem gait intact. Romberg negative. However, she bumps into door frames on her left side when walking through the clinic.
neurological_exam.additional: Balint syndrome screening: Simultanagnosia present — when shown a complex scene (Boston Cookie Theft picture), she describes individual elements but cannot integrate the whole scene. Oculomotor apraxia suspected — difficulty generating voluntary saccades to command, though pursuit movements are intact. Ideomotor apraxia testing: mild difficulty pantomiming use of a hammer and a toothbrush with preserved ability to imitate and use actual objects. No paratonic rigidity. No grasp reflex. No myoclonus.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive difficulty reading and navigating familiar environments over the past 18 months
patient.history_present_illness: Dr. Yuki Hasegawa is a 65-year-old right-handed retired civil engineer who presents to the neurology outpatient clinic with her husband for evaluation of progressive difficulty with reading, driving, and spatial orientation. Her husband reports that the first concerning change emerged approximately 18 months ago when she began having trouble reading engineering blueprints she was reviewing as a consultant — she described the lines as 'jumping around' and felt unable to follow them across the page. Around the same time, she was noted to have difficulty judging distances while parking the car, frequently misjudging the space and scraping the wheels against curbs. Over the subsequent months, she developed progressive difficulty reading books, stating that she loses her place on the page and cannot track lines of text, despite having had successful cataract surgery three years prior with good corrected visual acuity. Her ophthalmologist performed a thorough examination six months ago and found her visual acuity to be 20/25 in both eyes with normal intraocular pressures and healthy retinae, concluding there was no ophthalmological explanation for her symptoms. She has since stopped driving entirely after a frightening incident four months ago in which she failed to notice a car approaching from her left at an intersection. Her husband reports that she has also become hesitant navigating their condominium, occasionally reaching past objects she is trying to grasp, such as door handles or cups on a table. She has difficulty dressing, particularly buttoning shirts and clasping her bra, tasks she attributes to 'clumsiness' rather than visual difficulty. She has gotten disoriented twice in a shopping mall she has visited for decades, unable to find her way back to the entrance. Notably, her memory for recent events and conversations appears relatively well preserved. She remembers appointments, follows the news, and can recount details of recent family events accurately. Her husband confirms that she does not repeat questions or forget conversations. Her personality and behavior are unchanged — she remains socially engaged, empathetic, and maintains her sense of humor. She has no hallucinations, no tremor, no gait instability on flat surfaces, and no incontinence. She has become increasingly frustrated and anxious about her visual and spatial difficulties, and she is concerned she may be developing macular degeneration despite reassurance from her eye doctor.

findings.location: Bilateral parieto-occipital cortex, right greater than left

signal_characteristics.T1: Widened sulci and prominent cortical thinning in bilateral parieto-occipital regions, asymmetrically affecting the right hemisphere
signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: No signal abnormality within atrophic cortex
Confluent periventricular caps and extending rims with scattered deep white matter foci, moderate for age

signal_characteristics.Coronal_T1: Hippocampal volumes appear age-appropriate, Scheltens MTA score 1 bilaterally

findings.location: Bilateral posterior temporal and inferior parietal lobules

signal_characteristics.T1: Mildly widened sulci in posterior temporal and angular gyrus regions bilaterally

volumetrics.hippocampal_volume: Scheltens MTA score 1 bilaterally — mild, within age-appropriate range
volumetrics.ventricular_system: Mild prominence of the lateral ventricles, possibly slightly disproportionate to degree of cortical atrophy posteriorly
volumetrics.cortical_thickness: Reduced cortical thickness in bilateral parieto-occipital regions, right greater than left; frontal and anterior temporal cortex relatively preserved

additional_observations: Old lacunar infarct in the left caudate nucleus
No evidence of acute hemorrhage on SWI
Two microbleeds noted on SWI: one in the right parietal lobe, one in the left frontal subcortical white matter
Patent major intracranial vessels on MRA
mri.impression: Posterior-predominant cortical atrophy involving the parieto-occipital regions bilaterally, right greater than left, with relative preservation of hippocampal volumes. Moderate white matter disease (Fazekas grade 2) and a chronic lacunar infarct in the left caudate head suggest concurrent small vessel disease. Two cerebral microbleeds are noted. The pattern of posterior-predominant atrophy with preserved hippocampi is atypical for typical Alzheimer disease and could represent posterior cortical atrophy, vascular contributions to cognitive impairment, or a combination. Clinical correlation is recommended.

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: Posterior cortical atrophy (atypical Alzheimer disease variant)
differential_by_imaging.likelihood: Moderate — posterior-predominant pattern is suggestive but vascular disease confounds
Moderate — Fazekas grade 2 white matter disease, lacunar infarct, and microbleeds suggest significant vascular burden

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: Lewy body dementia with posterior cortical involvement
differential_by_imaging.likelihood: Low — would need clinical correlation for core features
Low — asymmetric posterior atrophy could fit but typically more frontoparietal

recommended_actions: Clinical correlation with detailed neuropsychological testing emphasizing visuospatial and visuoperceptual domains
Consider FDG-PET to determine metabolic pattern
Consider amyloid PET or CSF biomarkers to assess for underlying Alzheimer pathology
Follow-up MRI in 12 months to assess progression

BMP.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated fasting glucose, consistent with known type 2 diabetes mellitus

Thyroid.clinical_significance: Normal thyroid function; hypothyroid cognitive impairment excluded

Dementia_workup.clinical_significance: Reflects diabetes management; elevated HbA1c is a vascular risk factor but does not explain the posterior cortical syndrome
Normal; argues against inflammatory or vasculitic process

Lipid_Panel.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated LDL despite statin therapy; suboptimal cardiovascular risk control

labs.interpretation: Laboratory workup is largely unremarkable for reversible causes of cognitive impairment. Thyroid function is normal, vitamin B12 and folate are adequate, and infectious etiologies (syphilis, HIV) have been excluded. Inflammatory markers are within normal limits. The mildly elevated fasting glucose and HbA1c of 6.8% reflect the patient's known type 2 diabetes. LDL cholesterol is mildly elevated. These vascular risk factors are noteworthy given the white matter disease and lacunar infarct on MRI, but do not explain the progressive posterior cortical syndrome.
abnormal_values_summary: Glucose 118 mg/dL (elevated, consistent with known diabetes)
LDL 112 mg/dL (mildly elevated despite statin therapy)

Neuropsychological_Battery.clinical_significance: Within normal range; MMSE is insensitive to posterior cortical dysfunction
Below cutoff; losses primarily on visuospatial/executive (cube copy 0/1, clock 1/3, trails 0/1) and abstraction (1/2) items

Neuropsychological_Battery.test: Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Test - Immediate Recall
Neuropsychological_Battery.clinical_significance: Verbal learning and immediate memory within normal limits

Neuropsychological_Battery.test: Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Test - Delayed Recall
Neuropsychological_Battery.clinical_significance: Delayed verbal recall preserved, unusual for typical Alzheimer disease
Severely impaired visuoconstructional ability; piecemeal approach without appreciation of global structure — hallmark of simultanagnosia
Impaired visual memory, but poor copy quality limits interpretation
Severely impaired visuospatial perception, consistent with posterior cortical dysfunction

Neuropsychological_Battery.test: Visual Object and Space Perception Battery - Object Decision
Neuropsychological_Battery.clinical_significance: Impaired visual object recognition from unusual angles
Slowed, likely due to visuospatial search demands rather than primary processing speed deficit
Impaired, reflecting visuospatial demands compounded with set-shifting
Mild depressive symptoms, likely reactive to functional decline; does not account for the visuospatial deficits

output.interpretation: Comprehensive neuropsychological testing reveals a striking dissociation between preserved episodic verbal memory (RAVLT delayed recall 35th percentile) and severely impaired visuospatial and visuoperceptual functions (Rey Complex Figure copy at 2nd percentile, Judgment of Line Orientation at 3rd percentile, VOSP Object Decision at 5th percentile). Language functions including naming and fluency are intact. This profile is not consistent with typical amnestic Alzheimer disease, which presents with prominent memory impairment. Instead, this pattern is characteristic of posterior cortical atrophy (PCA), a syndrome most commonly caused by Alzheimer disease pathology targeting the parieto-occipital cortex. The preserved memory with devastated visuospatial function is the hallmark cognitive signature of PCA. Mild depressive symptoms are present but do not explain the visuospatial deficits. The relatively normal MMSE (27/30) highlights the insensitivity of this screening tool to posterior cortical dysfunction.
abnormal_values_summary: Rey Complex Figure Copy at 2nd percentile — severely impaired
Judgment of Line Orientation at 3rd percentile — severely impaired
VOSP Object Decision at 5th percentile — impaired
Trail Making A at 10th percentile, B at 8th percentile — impaired

findings.location: Diffuse cortical uptake, particularly prominent in posterior parietal, occipital, and lateral temporal cortex

signal_characteristics.Distribution: Diffuse bilateral cortical binding with notable posterior predominance involving parietal and occipital association cortex

additional_observations: Posterior-predominant distribution of amyloid binding is consistent with the atypical posterior cortical atrophy variant of Alzheimer disease
No focal asymmetry beyond the expected right-greater-than-left posterior pattern
output.impression: POSITIVE amyloid PET scan. Diffuse cortical amyloid deposition with a posterior-predominant distribution, most marked in parietal and occipital association cortex. This pattern, in the clinical context of progressive visuospatial dysfunction with preserved episodic memory, strongly supports a diagnosis of posterior cortical atrophy due to underlying Alzheimer disease pathology.

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: Posterior cortical atrophy due to Alzheimer disease

differential_by_imaging.likelihood: Excluded by clinical syndrome of progressive posterior cortical dysfunction

recommended_actions: Integrate with neuropsychological profile and structural MRI for definitive PCA-AD diagnosis
Discuss diagnosis and prognosis with patient and family

findings.location: Bilateral parieto-occipital cortex, right greater than left, extending into posterior temporal cortex

signal_characteristics.FDG_PET: Markedly reduced FDG uptake in bilateral parietal and occipital association cortices, with relative preservation of medial temporal and frontal lobe metabolism
signal_characteristics.Pattern: Posterior-predominant hypometabolism with sparing of primary visual cortex (V1), medial temporal structures, and prefrontal cortex

additional_observations: Medial temporal lobe metabolism is preserved, which is atypical for classic Alzheimer disease but characteristic of the posterior cortical atrophy variant
Primary visual cortex (calcarine region) shows relatively preserved metabolism
Precuneus and posterior cingulate show reduced metabolism, a pattern overlapping with typical AD
Frontal metabolism is preserved, arguing against frontotemporal dementia
Occipital association cortex hypometabolism is more prominent than typically seen in Lewy body dementia
output.impression: FDG-PET reveals a posterior-predominant pattern of cortical hypometabolism centered on the parieto-occipital association cortex bilaterally (right greater than left), with relative sparing of medial temporal, frontal, and primary visual cortex. This metabolic signature is characteristic of posterior cortical atrophy, most commonly due to Alzheimer disease pathology. The pattern is distinct from typical AD (which shows temporoparietal and medial temporal hypometabolism), Lewy body dementia (which shows occipital including primary visual cortex involvement), and frontotemporal dementia.

differential_by_imaging.likelihood: Low — primary visual cortex preserved, and occipital pattern is association cortex predominant

recommended_actions: Correlate with amyloid PET or CSF biomarkers to confirm Alzheimer pathology

output.interpretation: CSF biomarkers show equivocal results that complicate the diagnostic picture. Amyloid beta-42 is decreased at 420 pg/mL and the Abeta42/40 ratio is reduced at 0.054, indicating probable amyloid positivity (A+). However, phosphorylated tau is only borderline elevated at 62 pg/mL and total tau is within normal limits at 380 pg/mL, yielding an ambiguous tau/neurodegeneration status (T borderline, N-). This A+T(borderline)N- profile could represent early-stage Alzheimer pathology where tau-mediated neurodegeneration has not yet fully manifested in CSF, or could reflect mixed pathology. Prion disease markers are negative. The somewhat equivocal CSF profile, combined with the atypical clinical presentation and significant vascular disease on MRI, makes definitive diagnosis challenging on CSF biomarkers alone. Amyloid PET may provide more definitive evidence of amyloid pathology.

APOE_Genotyping.clinical_significance: Homozygous APOE e3. No increased genetic risk for late-onset Alzheimer disease conferred by APOE genotype. However, APOE e4 is neither necessary nor sufficient for AD diagnosis, and its absence does not exclude AD.

Early_Onset_AD_Panel.clinical_significance: Presenilin-1 mutations are the most common cause of autosomal dominant early-onset AD
Presenilin-2 mutations are a rare cause of familial AD
Amyloid precursor protein mutations are a rare cause of familial AD
Progranulin mutations cause frontotemporal dementia, included given atypical presentation

output.interpretation: Genetic testing reveals APOE E3/E3 homozygosity, which does not confer increased risk for late-onset Alzheimer disease. Given the patient's relatively young age (65) and atypical presentation, an early-onset dementia gene panel was performed. No pathogenic variants were identified in PSEN1, PSEN2, APP, or GRN. The absence of APOE e4 and negative early-onset panel do not exclude Alzheimer disease as the etiology; the majority of AD cases, including PCA variant, are sporadic. Genetic counseling has been offered.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9 Hz well-formed alpha activity over occipital regions, within normal limits
background.reactivity: PDR attenuates appropriately with eye opening

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: No focal or generalized abnormalities produced
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Normal photic driving response; no photoparoxysmal response

output.impression: Normal EEG. The posterior dominant rhythm is 9 Hz and well-formed. No focal or generalized slowing. No epileptiform discharges. No electrographic seizures. A normal EEG does not exclude early neurodegenerative disease, particularly posterior cortical atrophy where the EEG may remain normal until later stages.
output.limitations: Routine 30-minute recording in the awake state.
recommended_actions: EEG does not contribute to the diagnostic workup at this time
Consider repeat EEG if clinical course suggests rapid progression to evaluate for prion disease or other rapidly progressive dementias

interactions: No significant interactions with amlodipine, metformin, rosuvastatin, or aspirin
warnings: May cause bradycardia; baseline ECG recommended given cardiovascular risk factors
GI side effects (nausea, diarrhea) common at initiation; consider taking with evening meal
Metformin and donepezil together may increase GI intolerance; monitor for nausea and anorexia
Avoid concurrent anticholinergic medications which would reduce efficacy
alternatives: Rivastigmine patch — may have fewer GI side effects than oral cholinesterase inhibitors

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Posterior cortical atrophy (atypical Alzheimer's disease variant)

differential.likelihood: Moderate initially, low after biomarker confirmation
differential.key_distinguishing: Fazekas grade 2 white matter disease, lacunar infarct, and vascular risk factors suggest significant vascular burden; however, the progressive posterior cortical syndrome with visuospatial predominance is not typical of vascular cognitive impairment, which usually presents with executive dysfunction and stepwise decline

Visual symptoms could suggest LBD, but the patient has no visual hallucinations, no parkinsonism, no REM sleep behavior disorder, and no cognitive fluctuations; the visuospatial deficits in PCA are higher-order cortical deficits, not the visuoperceptual hallucinations of LBD

Asymmetric cortical dysfunction with apraxia and cortical sensory loss could suggest CBD; however, the absence of rigidity, dystonia, alien limb, and myoclonus argues against it; the visuospatial predominance favors PCA over CBD

Mild reactive depressive symptoms present but do not explain the objective visuospatial deficits; progressive 18-month course with structural and metabolic imaging correlates excludes a purely psychiatric etiology
Already excluded by comprehensive ophthalmological examination showing 20/25 acuity and normal retinal examination; the visual symptoms are central (cortical) in origin, not peripheral

optimal_actions.action: Obtain detailed history from patient and informant, specifically characterizing the nature of visual and spatial complaints versus memory complaints
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Progressive visuospatial dysfunction (reading difficulty, navigation problems, optic ataxia) with relatively preserved episodic memory — atypical for classic AD

optimal_actions.action: Perform comprehensive neurological examination with emphasis on higher cortical visual functions including simultanagnosia, optic ataxia, oculomotor apraxia, visual extinction, and cortical sensory testing
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Features of Balint syndrome (simultanagnosia, optic ataxia, oculomotor apraxia) and Gerstmann syndrome elements; visual extinction on double simultaneous stimulation; impaired graphesthesia and stereognosis; relatively preserved memory on bedside testing

optimal_actions.action: Order dementia screening labs including CBC, BMP, TSH, vitamin B12, folate, RPR, HIV, and additional vascular risk markers given imaging findings
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal except for markers of known diabetes (elevated glucose, HbA1c) and mildly elevated LDL; reversible causes of cognitive impairment excluded

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI with volumetric protocol
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Posterior-predominant parieto-occipital atrophy with preserved hippocampal volumes; concurrent vascular disease (Fazekas 2, lacunar infarct) creating diagnostic ambiguity

optimal_actions.action: Request comprehensive neuropsychological testing with emphasis on visuospatial, visuoperceptual, and visuoconstructional domains
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Severely impaired visuospatial and visuoconstructional abilities with preserved episodic verbal memory — the cognitive signature of posterior cortical atrophy

optimal_actions.action: Order FDG-PET to characterize the metabolic pattern and differentiate between neurodegenerative etiologies
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Posterior-predominant parieto-occipital hypometabolism with preserved medial temporal metabolism — characteristic PCA pattern

optimal_actions.action: Order amyloid PET to confirm underlying Alzheimer pathology given the atypical presentation and confounding vascular disease
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Positive amyloid PET confirming Alzheimer disease as the underlying pathology of the posterior cortical atrophy syndrome

optimal_actions.action: Consider initiating cholinesterase inhibitor therapy (donepezil 5 mg daily) and check drug interactions
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No significant drug interactions with current medications

optimal_actions.action: Refer to low-vision rehabilitation and occupational therapy for adaptive strategies for visuospatial deficits
Counsel patient and family on diagnosis of PCA as an atypical variant of Alzheimer disease, discuss prognosis, safety planning (driving cessation confirmed), and advance directives

critical_actions: Recognize that the visuospatial-predominant presentation with preserved memory is atypical for classic AD and consider posterior cortical atrophy
Perform detailed higher cortical visual function testing (simultanagnosia, optic ataxia, oculomotor apraxia) — do not rely on MMSE alone
Obtain ophthalmological evaluation to exclude primary visual pathology (already done in this case)
Do not dismiss symptoms as normal aging or attribute them solely to vascular disease despite MRI findings of white matter disease
Order amyloid PET or CSF biomarkers to confirm Alzheimer pathology underlying the PCA syndrome
Confirm driving cessation given the severity of visuospatial impairment
Refer to low-vision rehabilitation services specialized in cortical visual impairment
contraindicated_actions: Attributing all symptoms to vascular disease and treating only vascular risk factors without investigating neurodegenerative etiology
Prescribing anticholinergic medications (e.g., oxybutynin, diphenhydramine) which worsen cognitive function
Relying on MMSE score of 27/30 to conclude cognition is normal — MMSE is insensitive to posterior cortical dysfunction
Recommending cataract revision or other ophthalmological procedures for what is a cortical visual processing deficit
Prescribing benzodiazepines for anxiety without considering cognitive side effects
Allowing continued driving given the severity of visuospatial deficits and documented near-miss incident
Starting memantine monotherapy (not indicated as first-line in mild disease)
key_reasoning_points: Posterior cortical atrophy (PCA) is an atypical variant of Alzheimer disease that presents with progressive visuospatial dysfunction rather than memory loss, making it a diagnostic challenge
The combination of simultanagnosia, optic ataxia, and oculomotor apraxia constitutes Balint syndrome, which localizes to bilateral parieto-occipital cortex
Preserved episodic memory (RAVLT delayed recall 35th percentile) with devastated visuospatial function (Rey Complex Figure copy 2nd percentile) is the hallmark neuropsychological signature of PCA
MMSE is insensitive to PCA — a score of 27/30 can mask severe posterior cortical dysfunction because the test emphasizes orientation, memory, and language over visuospatial abilities

The MRI creates diagnostic ambiguity: posterior-predominant atrophy is consistent with PCA, but the Fazekas grade 2 white matter disease, lacunar infarct, and microbleeds suggest vascular contributions that could be mistaken for the primary diagnosis
Amyloid PET is the key investigation that resolves the diagnostic puzzle by confirming Alzheimer pathology as the substrate of the posterior cortical syndrome
The absence of APOE e4 does not exclude Alzheimer disease — most sporadic AD cases, and particularly PCA, do not require genetic risk alleles

CSF biomarkers in this case are equivocal (A+ but T borderline, N-), reflecting either early disease stage or the known lower sensitivity of CSF markers in atypical AD presentations, further underscoring the diagnostic complexity

key_educational_points: PCA is the most common atypical variant of Alzheimer disease, accounting for approximately 5% of AD cases
Patients with PCA frequently present to ophthalmologists before neurologists, leading to diagnostic delay
The MMSE and similar brief screening tools are insensitive to posterior cortical dysfunction and should not be relied upon to rule out significant cognitive impairment
Vascular disease on MRI can be a red herring that diverts attention from the underlying neurodegenerative process
Amyloid PET and FDG-PET are particularly valuable in atypical dementia presentations where structural MRI findings are ambiguous
Low-vision rehabilitation is a critical but often overlooked referral for PCA patients
